content:
# 2 Corinthians Chapter 2 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

---

## 2:1 — "not to come again to you in grief" vs. "not to make another painful visit"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "not to come again to you in grief" | Literal μὴ πάλιν ἐν λύπῃ πρὸς ὑμᾶς ἐλθεῖν |
| LOGOS | "not to make another painful visit to you" | "Painful visit" as English idiom |
| PNEUMA | "not make another painful visit to you" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS/PNEUMA. "Painful visit" captures the idiom naturally while preserving the meaning.

**Status:** Resolved. Minor stylistic difference.

---

## 2:4 — "through many tears" — structural divergence

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "out of much affliction and anguish of heart I wrote to you through many tears" | Preserves Greek clause order |
| LOGOS | "I wrote to you out of much affliction and anguish of heart and with many tears" | Reordered for English |
| PNEUMA | "I wrote that letter in great anguish, with a troubled heart and many tears" | "That letter" (interpretive addition); "troubled heart" for "anguish of heart" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA's "that letter" adds specificity not in the Greek (though contextually justified). "Troubled heart" softens "anguish of heart."

**Status:** Resolved. PNEUMA adds interpretive context.

---

## 2:5 — "in part — that I may not burden — all of you"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he has not grieved me, but in part — that I may not burden — all of you" | Preserves the parenthetical structure and ἐπιβαρῶ |
| LOGOS | "he has caused it not to me, but in some measure — not to put it too severely — to all of you" | "In some measure" for ἀπὸ μέρους; "not to put it too severely" for ἵνα μὴ ἐπιβαρῶ |
| PNEUMA | "I am not overstating it when I say that the man who caused all the trouble hurt all of you more than he hurt me" | Full restructuring; interpretive expansion |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. This verse has notoriously difficult syntax. LOGOS captures the diplomatic hedging ("in some measure," "not to put it too severely"). PNEUMA restructures entirely, adding "the man who caused all the trouble" and reversing the emphasis.

**Status:** Resolved. Syntactically difficult verse — all three handle it differently.

---

## 2:6 — "punishment by the many" vs. "punishment by the majority" vs. "most of you opposed him"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "this punishment by the many" | ἡ ἐπιτιμία αὕτη ὑπὸ τῶν πλειόνων |
| LOGOS | "this punishment by the majority" | τῶν πλειόνων as "majority" |
| PNEUMA | "Most of you opposed him, and that was punishment enough" | Full paraphrase; "opposed" replaces "punishment" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. τῶν πλειόνων ("the majority") reveals congregational governance. PNEUMA's "opposed" replaces the specific term ἐπιτιμία ("punishment/censure") with a vaguer word.

**Status:** Resolved. PNEUMA softens the disciplinary language.

---

## 2:10 — "in the face of Christos" vs. "in the presence of Christ" vs. "with Christ's authority"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "for your sake in [the] face of Christos" | Literal ἐν προσώπῳ Χριστοῦ |
| LOGOS | "for your sake in the presence of Christ" | "Presence" for πρόσωπον |
| PNEUMA | "I do so with Christ's authority for your benefit" | "Authority" — interpretive; "benefit" for "sake" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. ἐν προσώπῳ can mean "in the presence of" or "in the person of." "Presence" is the most natural reading. PNEUMA's "authority" imports a concept not directly in the phrase.

**Status:** Resolved. PNEUMA's "authority" is interpretation beyond the text.

---

## 2:11 — "Satanas" vs. "Satan" — and "schemes" vs. "designs" vs. "evil schemes"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the Satanas; for we are not ignorant of his schemes" | Transliterated name; "schemes" for νοήματα |
| LOGOS | "Satan; for we are not ignorant of his designs" | Standard name; "designs" for νοήματα |
| PNEUMA | "Satan will not outsmart us. For we are familiar with his evil schemes" | "Outsmart" for πλεονεκτηθῶμεν; adds "evil" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. "Designs" is a precise rendering of νοήματα. PNEUMA adds "evil" (not in Greek) and uses "outsmart" — while πλεονεκτέω means "take advantage of/defraud," "outsmart" narrows the sense.

**Status:** Resolved. PNEUMA adds interpretive adjective and narrowing verb choice.

---

## 2:13 — "I had no rest in the spirit of me" vs. "my spirit was not at rest" vs. "I had no peace of mind"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I had no rest in the spirit of me" | Literal τῷ πνεύματί μου |
| LOGOS | "my spirit was not at rest" | Smooth English preserving "spirit" |
| PNEUMA | "I had no peace of mind" | "Peace of mind" replaces "rest in spirit" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. "Spirit" (πνεῦμα) is more than "mind" — it denotes Paul's inner self at a deeper level than cognitive function. PNEUMA's "peace of mind" reduces the anthropological term.

**Status:** Resolved. Anthropologically significant — πνεῦμα vs. "mind."

---

## 2:14 — "leading us in triumphal procession" — key metaphor

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the [one] always leading us in triumphal procession in the Christos and making manifest the fragrance" | θριαμβεύοντι preserved; participial structure |
| LOGOS | "who in Christ always leads us in triumphal procession, and through us spreads the fragrance" | Same metaphor; cleaner syntax |
| PNEUMA | "He has made us his captives and continues to lead us along in Christ's triumphal procession" | Adds "his captives" — makes the subversive implication explicit |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA's addition of "his captives" makes explicit what the Greek implies (in a Roman triumph, those led were captives), but Paul's text leaves the reader's role ambiguous — participant or captive? The ambiguity is productive.

**Status:** Resolved. Theologically significant — PNEUMA resolves an intentional ambiguity.

---

## 2:15-16 — "sweet aroma" vs. "aroma of Christ" vs. "Christ-like fragrance"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a sweet aroma we are to God...an odor from death to death...an odor from life to life" | "Sweet aroma" (εὐωδία); "odor" (ὀσμή) |
| LOGOS | "we are the aroma of Christ to God...a fragrance from death to death...a fragrance from life to life" | "Aroma of Christ"; "fragrance" for both |
| PNEUMA | "Our lives are a Christ-like fragrance rising up to God...a dreadful smell of death and doom...a life-giving perfume" | "Christ-like fragrance"; "dreadful smell"; "perfume" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. EMET's distinction between "sweet aroma" and "odor" is lost in English (both Greek words denote smell). PNEUMA's "dreadful smell of death and doom" and "life-giving perfume" are vivid but import emotional valence not in the Greek — the text simply says "from death to death" and "from life to life."

**Status:** Resolved. PNEUMA adds emotional coloring to a deliberately stark metaphor.

---

## 2:17 — "peddling" vs. "peddlers" vs. "hucksters"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "peddling the word of God" | καπηλεύοντες as participle |
| LOGOS | "peddlers of God's word" | Noun form |
| PNEUMA | "the many hucksters who preach for personal profit" | "Hucksters"; adds "for personal profit" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. "Peddlers" is precise. PNEUMA's "hucksters" is colloquial and "for personal profit" is an interpretive addition (though historically accurate).

**Status:** Resolved. PNEUMA adds explanatory material.

---

*Summary: 10 disagreements logged. No textual variants — all disagreements are word choice, structural, or interpretive. Most significant: v.10 (πρόσωπον as "presence" vs. "authority"), v.13 (πνεῦμα as "spirit" vs. "mind"), v.14 (whether to make the "captive" implication explicit), v.15-16 (emotional coloring of the aroma metaphor).*

— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA
